Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd tell myself I'd study every evening, but then I'd just feel too drained to actually follow through. I spent a lot of time watching lecture videos and scrolling through course materials, but actually sitting down to complete assignments felt overwhelming. I'd usually end up studying in bursts when I felt slightly less anxious, which wasn't often enough.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on how I was feeling emotionally that week. Some weeks I'd manage to study for several hours, other weeks I could barely bring myself to open my laptop. Deadlines would force me into action, but it was always last-minute panic rather than planned effort. The inconsistency really frustrated me because I knew I was capable of more.
Q3: I logged into the LMS pretty frequently, actually—probably out of anxiety more than anything productive. I'd check if there were new announcements or if I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd just browse through the materials to feel like I was doing something, even when I wasn't really absorbing anything. The gamified tasks would sometimes pull me in because they felt less intimidating than regular assignments.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became almost compulsive for me. Every morning I'd log in with my coffee, scroll through the dashboard, maybe watch part of a video. It became routine, but not necessarily productive routine—more like a comfort habit that made me feel connected to the course even when I wasn't really engaging deeply. The points and badges from the gamified elements kept me coming back.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewatch confusing parts. I watched over four hours of video content because seeing concepts illustrated really helped them click for me. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students explained things in different ways than the instructor. Visual diagrams and infographics in the course materials were particularly useful.
Q6: I avoided the readings and text-heavy PDFs a lot. My eyes would just glaze over, and with my anxiety being so high, I couldn't concentrate on dense text for long. I also minimally engaged with the actual assignments—my completion rate was terrible, I know. It wasn't that I didn't understand the importance; I just felt paralyzed when it came to producing work that would be graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it didn't trigger my anxiety too much and if I could see immediate progress. The gamified tasks were good for this—earning points or badges gave me a small dopamine hit that made me feel like I'd accomplished something. Activities that broke content into smaller, manageable chunks felt less overwhelming than big assignments.
Q8: Optional activities actually felt safer to me in some ways because there was no pressure of being judged. I could explore them without the fear of failing. Required activities made my anxiety spike because I knew they'd affect my grade. I'd often do the optional gamified stuff while procrastinating on the required assignments, which probably wasn't the best strategy.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and visual materials to my tablet so I could review them without being online. I also used YouTube videos and Khan Academy to supplement concepts I didn't understand. I have access to good resources at home, so I'd often study from textbooks and printed notes while lying in bed because sitting at my desk felt too formal and stressful.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be reviewing downloaded slides or rewatching videos I'd saved offline, trying to understand concepts. None of that showed up anywhere. I also spent time making visual mind maps and color-coded notes by hand, which really helped me process information. My LMS activity would show nothing during those hours even though I was genuinely trying to learn.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, then spend days returning to it offline. It was easier on my eyes to read printed materials, and I could study in bed when my anxiety made sitting at my desk unbearable. The LMS would just show one download, but that material might have been my main study resource for a whole week.
Q12: There were several weeks where everything felt impossible. My anxiety would get so bad that even opening the LMS made my heart race. When assignments piled up, I'd freeze completely instead of tackling them one by one. I felt sad most of the time, and that heaviness made it hard to care about coursework even though part of me desperately wanted to succeed.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload from seven online courses was crushing, and I couldn't keep up. When I was exhausted or confused, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities weren't even the main issue—it was more the mental exhaustion from constantly battling my own anxiety that drained me.
Q14: Many times. I'd have the course page open for hours, technically 'accessing' materials, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about everything I hadn't done, feeling guilty, spiraling. I'd watch videos but realize at the end that I'd absorbed nothing because I was too anxious to focus. It was like being physically present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even though I knew they were destroying my grade. The thought of starting them triggered such intense anxiety that avoidance felt like the only way to cope in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same or worse. It was a vicious cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that could override my avoidance, but by then it was usually too late to do quality work. I'd panic-submit something incomplete or not submit at all because the deadline had passed. The LMS would show me logging in frantically near due dates, which probably looked like procrastination but was really anxiety paralysis finally breaking.
Q17: Feedback was a double-edged sword. When it was encouraging, it motivated me briefly. But critical feedback, even constructive criticism, would send me into a spiral of feeling worthless and incapable. After getting a low score, I'd avoid the LMS for days because I couldn't face seeing my failures. The gamified positive reinforcement was gentler and kept me more engaged.
Q18: The course structure with so many components felt overwhelming to me. Having seven online courses meant constant notifications and deadlines from multiple directions. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized visually, but the sheer volume of content made it hard to know where to focus. I often felt lost about what I should prioritize.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, less threatening tasks. But the assignment structure made things harder—big projects with distant deadlines gave my anxiety too much room to grow. Clearer visual roadmaps of what to do and when would have helped me a lot. The discussion forums were accessible, which is why I participated there.
Q20: That describes me pretty well, actually. I was highly active in terms of logins, watching videos, and participating in discussions, but my assignment completion was abysmal. For me, it was anxiety blocking the transition from consuming content to producing work. Engaging with materials felt safe; submitting work for judgment felt terrifying. The gap between activity and output was my anxiety made visible.
Q21: I think some students are just more efficient or better at offline studying. They might download everything once, study from printed materials or their own notes, and only return to submit work. That's partially my experience too—I did a lot of learning that never showed up in logs. But my low submission rate meant my performance didn't reflect my actual understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show frequency and patterns of access, time spent on videos, and participation in discussions—surface-level engagement. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement, whether someone is actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or the difference between productive and anxiety-driven activity.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just clicking through it. It's when concepts stick with you and you find yourself thinking about them outside of study time. For me, true engagement happened in quiet moments with my handwritten notes, not necessarily when I was logged into the LMS. It's more about mental investment than digital activity.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I was struggling emotionally while still trying to stay connected to the course. It can't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the genuine interest I had in the material despite my terrible assignment completion. It also can't show that my high login frequency was often driven by anxiety rather than productive engagement, or how sad and overwhelmed I felt most of the time.
